title: How to Stop Second-Guessing Your Intuition
meta_description: Stop second guessing your intuition with this direct guide. Learn to trust your gut, quiet doubt, and recognize real psychic hits. No fluff.
og_title: How to Stop Second-Guessing Your Intuition
og_description: Stop second guessing your intuition with this direct guide. Learn to trust your gut, quiet doubt, and recognize real psychic hits. No fluff.
body:
You Keep Asking "Was That Real or Was I Making It Up?"

You get a hit. A quiet knowing. A gut pull that says "don't take that job" or "call your sister right now" or "turn left instead of right." And then, within seconds, the second-guessing starts.

"That was probably just anxiety."

"I'm making that up because I'm scared."

"There's no way I could actually know that."

You talk yourself out of it. You ignore it. You go with the logical option instead. And then, hours or days later, you find out you were right. The job would've been a nightmare. Your sister needed you. That left turn would've saved you thirty minutes of traffic.

And you feel that familiar mix of relief and frustration. Relief that you're not crazy. Frustration that you didn't trust yourself.

Sound familiar? Right?!

Here's the thing nobody tells you about intuition: it's not a magical superpower that some people have and others don't. It's a biological signal. A real, measurable communication between your body, your nervous system, and the information you're picking up from the world around you. The problem isn't that you don't have intuition. The problem is that you've been trained to override it.

Let's talk about how to stop doing that.

Why You Second-Guess Yourself in the First Place

Before we get into the how-to part, you need to understand why this is happening. Because if you don't know the root cause, you'll just keep adding techniques on top of a shaky foundation.

The most common reason you second-guess your intuition is that you were raised in a culture that values logic, data, and external proof over internal knowing. From the time you were a kid, you were told to "think before you act" and "be reasonable" and "don't be so sensitive." You learned that your feelings were unreliable. That your gut was just your imagination. That the only trustworthy information came from outside yourself - from experts, from books, from people with credentials.

And if you're someone who feels things deeply - if you're an empath, a highly sensitive person, or just someone who's always been a little "too much" for other people - you got that message extra hard. You were told to calm down. To stop being dramatic. To get out of your head.

So you learned to distrust your own internal compass. You learned to second-guess everything. And now, as an adult, you're stuck in a loop where you get a clear signal, immediately doubt it, and then spend hours or days trying to figure out what's real.

Does that land?

The First Step: Separate Intuition from Fear

This is the biggest hurdle for most people. They can't tell the difference between a genuine intuitive hit and their own anxious thoughts. And honestly? That's not your fault. They feel similar in the body.

But they're not the same. Here's how you can tell them apart:

  • Intuition is calm. It feels like a quiet knowing. A certainty that doesn't need to argue with you. It doesn't come with a rush of adrenaline or a story about what could go wrong. It just is.
  • Fear is loud. It comes with a story. "But what if I'm wrong? What if I make a mistake? What if people think I'm crazy?" Fear loops. It repeats itself. It tries to convince you.
  • Intuition is neutral. It doesn't care if you follow it or not. It just shows up and waits. Fear, on the other hand, is desperate. It needs you to act or not act. It has a stake in the outcome.
  • Intuition is physical. You feel it in your body - a tightness in your chest, a pull in your gut, a shiver down your spine. Fear is in your head. It's a story you're telling yourself.

Next time you get a hit, pause for ten seconds. Don't analyze it. Just feel it. Is it calm or is it frantic? Is it a quiet knowing or a loud argument? That's your first clue.

"The moment you stop asking 'is this real?' and start asking 'what is this trying to tell me?', your intuition stops being a question and starts being a conversation."

Stop Treating Intuition Like a Magic 8-Ball

One of the biggest reasons you second-guess yourself is that you're expecting your intuition to be perfect. You want it to give you a clear, undeniable answer that you can't possibly misinterpret. You want it to be like a flashing neon sign that says "DO THIS" or "DON'T DO THAT."

But intuition doesn't work that way. It's not a yes/no machine. It's a compass, not a map. It gives you direction, not step-by-step instructions.

Think about it this way: if you're driving and your gut says "don't take this road," that doesn't mean you're supposed to know why. Maybe there's an accident ahead. Maybe there's construction. Maybe a deer is about to cross. Your intuition is picking up on information you can't consciously access, and it's giving you a simple signal: "not this way."

You don't need to know the reason. You just need to trust the signal.

But most of us don't do that. We demand proof. We want a full explanation before we'll act. And by the time we've analyzed it to death, the moment has passed and we're left wondering what might've happened.

If you want to stop second-guessing, you have to stop demanding that your intuition be a perfect, all-knowing oracle. It's not. It's a subtle nudge. And it's okay to act on a nudge without having all the answers.

Build Trust with Small Bets

You can't go from "I second-guess everything" to "I fully trust my intuition" overnight. That's like trying to run a marathon when you've never walked around the block. You have to build trust slowly, with small bets.

Here's what that looks like in practice:

Start with low-stakes decisions. Things where the outcome doesn't really matter. Like what to eat for lunch. Or which route to take to work. Or whether to watch a movie or read a book. Get a hit, act on it, and see what happens.

Keep a record. Write down the hit, what you did, and what happened. This is crucial because your brain has a negativity bias - it remembers the times you were wrong way more than the times you were right. Writing it down gives you proof that you're actually pretty good at this.

After a few weeks of small bets, move up to medium-stakes decisions. Like whether to reach out to a friend you've been thinking about. Or whether to buy something you've been eyeing. Or whether to say yes or no to a social invitation.

Notice the pattern. Your intuition is probably right more often than you think. You just haven't been paying attention to the hits that land.

As you build this track record, your confidence grows. And confidence is the antidote to second-guessing. When you have proof that your intuition works, the doubt has less room to take hold.

Learn to Recognize Your Intuition's Signature

Everyone's intuition feels different. For some people, it's a physical sensation - a knot in the stomach, a warmth in the chest, a tingling in the hands. For others, it's a mental impression - a sudden image, a phrase that pops into their head, a song that starts playing at the right moment. For others, it's an emotional shift - a sudden feeling of peace or unease that doesn't match the situation.

Your job is to figure out what your intuition feels like. What's its signature? How does it show up for you?

One way to do this is to look back at times when you followed your intuition and it worked out. What did you feel in that moment? What was the quality of the knowing? Was it a voice? A feeling? A body sensation? Write it down.

Then look at times when you ignored your intuition and regretted it. What did you feel right before you ignored it? That's also your intuition, just in a different form.

Once you know your signature, you can start to recognize it in real time. And when you recognize it, you can act on it before the second-guessing starts.

For a deeper dive into how your body communicates intuitive information, I highly recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk (See on Amazon - paid link). It's not a psychic development book, but it explains how your nervous system stores information that your conscious mind doesn't have access to. That's literally what intuition is - your body processing data that your brain hasn't caught up with yet.

Stop Asking "What If I'm Wrong?" and Start Asking "What If I'm Right?"

This is a simple reframe, but it's powerful. Most of us approach our intuition from a place of fear. We ask "what if I'm wrong?" and then we spiral into all the terrible things that could happen if we make a mistake.

But what if you flipped that question? What if you asked "what if I'm right?" What if you considered the possibility that your intuition is actually correct, and the risk is in ignoring it, not in following it?

Think about the times you've ignored your intuition and regretted it. The cost of ignoring it was real. You missed an opportunity. You stayed in a bad situation. You hurt someone you cared about. The cost of following it, on the other hand, was usually just a little bit of embarrassment or discomfort.

When you frame it that way, the risk of following your intuition is actually lower than the risk of ignoring it. You're not being reckless by trusting yourself. You're being smart.

So the next time you feel that familiar doubt creep in, ask yourself: "What if I'm right? What would I do if I knew this was true?" Then do that thing.

Give Yourself Permission to Be Wrong Sometimes

Here's a hard truth: your intuition won't be right 100% of the time. Nobody's is. And that's okay.

The problem isn't that your intuition is sometimes wrong. The problem is that you've decided that being wrong is unacceptable. You've made it mean something about you - that you're foolish, that you're gullible, that you can't trust yourself. And that's a lot of pressure to put on a biological signal that's just trying to help you work through a complex world.

When you give yourself permission to be wrong, something shifts. You stop needing your intuition to be perfect. You stop second-guessing every hit because you're terrified of making a mistake. You relax into the process of learning and adjusting, just like you would with any other skill.

Think about it like learning to cook. When you first start, you follow recipes exactly. Sometimes they turn out great. Sometimes they don't. But you don't quit cooking because you burned a batch of cookies. You adjust the temperature, try a different technique, and keep going. Over time, you develop a feel for it. You don't need the recipe anymore. You just know.

Intuition is the same way. You have to be willing to burn a few batches of cookies to develop the feel. And that means being okay with being wrong sometimes.

If you want to learn more about how to work with your intuition as a skill you can develop, check out The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker (See on Amazon - paid link). It's a practical, no-nonsense book about how your intuition keeps you safe, and why ignoring it is actually more dangerous than following it. De Becker doesn't use any woo-woo language - he just lays out the evidence that your gut knows things your brain doesn't.

Create a Simple Decision-Making Ritual

One of the best ways to stop second-guessing is to have a process you follow every time you get an intuitive hit. A ritual that moves you from "is this real?" to "okay, I'm acting on this" without getting stuck in the middle.

Here's a simple one you can try:

  1. Pause. When you get a hit, stop what you're doing. Take a breath. Don't react immediately.
  2. Feel. Notice where you feel it in your body. Is it in your gut? Your chest? Your throat? Just notice it without judging it.
  3. Name it. Say it out loud or write it down. "I'm getting a hit that I shouldn't take this meeting." "I'm feeling like I should call my mom." Naming it makes it real.
  4. Check it. Is it calm or frantic? If it's calm, it's probably intuition. If it's frantic, it's probably fear. Trust the calm one.
  5. Act. If it's calm, act on it. Don't overthink it. Just do the thing. If it's fear, don't act on it. Just let it pass.

That's it. Five steps. The whole thing takes less than a minute. And the more you practice it, the faster you get.

The key is to make it automatic. When you have a ritual, you don't have to decide whether to trust your intuition in the moment. You just follow the process. And the process leads you to action.

Stop Comparing Your Intuition to Other People's

You read about people who get crystal clear messages from their guides. Or people who see visions. Or people who just know things with absolute certainty. And you look at your own vague gut feelings and think "I'm doing it wrong."

You're not doing it wrong. You're just doing it differently.

Intuition comes in different flavors. Some people are visual - they see images or symbols. Some people are auditory - they hear words or phrases. Some people are kinesthetic - they feel it in their body. Some people are cognitive - they just suddenly know something without knowing how they know.

None of these is better than the others. They're just different channels. And the channel you have is the one that's right for you.

If you're constantly comparing your experience to someone else's, you're going to miss the signals that are actually coming through for you. You'll be looking for a vision when your intuition is actually giving you a gut feeling. You'll be waiting for a voice when your intuition is actually giving you a body sensation.

Trust your own channel. It works for you. It's not broken. It's just different from the person next to you.

For more on understanding the different ways intuition shows up, I recommend The Intuitive Way by Penney Peirce (See on Amazon - paid link). It's a practical guide that helps you identify your natural intuitive style and work with it, rather than trying to force yourself into someone else's model.

The Only Person Who Can Validate Your Intuition Is You

You've been trained to look outside yourself for confirmation. You want someone else to tell you that your hit was real. You want a sign from the universe. You want external proof that you're not just making it up.

But here's the thing: the more you look outside for validation, the weaker your internal compass gets. Every time you ask someone else "is this real?" you're teaching yourself that you can't trust your own knowing.

And the people you're asking? They don't have access to your intuition. They don't know what you're picking up on. They're just guessing, based on their own biases and fears. So you're essentially asking a stranger to validate something only you can feel.

The only person who can validate your intuition is you. And you do that by acting on it, seeing what happens, and learning from the results. Not by asking for permission beforehand.

This is scary at first. It feels vulnerable. What if you're wrong? What if you make a mistake? But the truth is, you're already making mistakes by ignoring your intuition. You're just not calling them mistakes because nobody else knows about them. You're suffering in silence, second-guessing yourself into paralysis.

Wouldn't it be better to take a risk, follow your gut, and find out what happens? Even if you're wrong sometimes, at least you're in the game. At least you're learning. At least you're building the muscle of trust.

Start Today. Start Small. Start Now.

You don't need to overhaul your entire life to stop second-guessing your intuition. You just need to take one small step today.

Pick one low-stakes decision you're facing right now. Maybe it's what to have for dinner. Maybe it's whether to go for a walk or watch TV. Maybe it's which email to respond to first. Get a hit, act on it, and see what happens. That's it. That's all you need to do.

Then do it again tomorrow. And the next day. And the next.
